agent1_snippets.content: ATP is the primary source of phosphate. Once the protein dephosphorylates ATP and auto-phosphorylates the tyrosine residues residing in TK, the phosphotyrosines serve as docking sites for other proteins involved in signal transduction. This process is a series of complex biochemical processes that assist the cell in cell differentiation, regulation of cell growth, and cell death. The biochemical pathway is depicted in Figure 1. The crucial role tyrosine kinase proteins play in cell division has led oncologists to use the protein as a target site for targeted cancer therapy in an attempt to decrease uncontrolled cell division. agent1_snippets.title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Tyrosine kinase enzymes (TKs) can be categorized into receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs), non-receptor tyrosine kinases (NRTKs), and a small group of dual-specificity kinases (DSK) which can phosphorylate serine, threonine, and tyrosine residues. RTKs are transmembrane receptor that includes vascular endothelial growth factor receptors (VEGFR), platelet-derived growth factor receptors (PDGFR), insulin receptor (InsR) family, and the ErbB receptor family, which includes epidermal growth factor receptors (EGFR) and the human epidermal growth factor receptor-2 (HER2). NRTKs are cytoplasmic proteins that consist of nine families, including Abl, Ack, Csk, Fak, Fes/Fer, Jak, Src, Syk/Zap70, and Tec, with the addition of Brl/Sik, Rak/Frk, Rlk/Txk, and Srm, which fall outside the nine defined families. The most notable example of DSKs is the mitogen-activated protein kinase kinases (MEKs), which are principally involved in the MAP pathways. [1] [4] [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Cellular Level
agent1_snippets.content: Regulation of the activity of RTKs and their downstream pathways is critical, given the vital cellular processes they modulate. Negative feedback mechanisms are varied and occur at different levels in the signaling networks. The simplest example is the direct activation of protein tyrosine phosphatases, which dephosphorylate the tyrosine residues on active RTKs. In some RTKs, like epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), downstream effector kinases PKC and MAPK phosphorylate threonine residues in the juxtamembrane region, inhibiting high-affinity ligand binding. In the MAPK/ERK pathway, MAPK phosphorylation of the upstream docking protein Gab1 and protein kinase Sos impairs their ability to interact with other proteins. In contrast, phosphorylation of upstream Raf kinase dampens its enzyme activity. All these mechanisms inhibit or terminate the cellular response to RTK activation. Alternatively, RTK activity can undergo attenuation by removing receptors from the cell surface. Active RTKs recruit Cbl, a ubiquitin ligase that tags the receptors for endocytosis and degradation via ubiquitination. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: As a whole, tyrosine kinases phosphorylate specific amino acids on substrate enzymes, which subsequently alter signal transduction leading to downstream changes in cellular biology. The downstream signal transduction set off by TKs can modify cell growth, migration, differentiation, apoptosis, and death. Constitutive activation or inhibition, either by mutations or other means, can lead to dysregulated signal cascades, potentially resulting in malignancy and other pathologies. [4] [40] Therefore, blocking these initial signals via TKIs can prevent the aberrant action of the mutated or dysfunctional TKs.

agent1_snippets.content: The intracellular signaling pathways associated with RTKs are triggered when adaptor proteins (eg, Grb2) or protein kinases bind to phosphotyrosines on the RTK or its associated docking protein and become phosphorylated. A discussion of how ligand binding results in these phosphotyrosine-binding sites is in the Mechanism section. Phosphotyrosine binding is permitted by the Src homology 2 (SH2) and phosphotyrosine-binding (PTB) domains on the adaptor proteins and protein kinases. Adaptor proteins also contain Src homology 3 (SH3) or WW domains that target polyproline regions on protein kinases and guanine nucleotide exchange factors. These allow for the formation of signaling complexes and further protein kinase activation. RTKs utilize several signaling pathways, including:

agent1_snippets.title: Vemurafenib -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: The kinase protein family, including MAP, RAS, RAF, MEK, and ERK, has a key role in regulating intracellular and extracellular signaling pathways that control cellular growth, differentiation, transformation, and apoptosis (programmed cell death). In normal cells, these kinases operate in a controlled cascade of phosphorylation and dephosphorylation in response to growth factors, eventually leading to the regulation of cellular growth and proliferation in an organized manner. However, mutations in the genes encoding these kinases can disrupt this regulation, resulting in uncontrolled cell growth, which can lead to the development of cancer.

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Related Testing
agent1_snippets.content: The detection of RTK gene mutations, amplifications, and rearrangements is of the utmost clinical importance, as they have significant therapeutic and prognostic implications. The DNA sequencing, fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), and immunohistochemistry (IHC) are the main laboratory tests that detect these gene alterations. DNA sequencing using the Sanger method continues to predominate in clinical labs, although the use of next-generation sequencing techniques is increasing. These high throughput methods use parallel sequencing to produce large volumes of short sequence data more quickly and at a lower cost than traditional methods. They are most useful in detecting single-nucleotide mutations. [17]

agent1_snippets.title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Treatment Planning
agent1_snippets.content: Any of the 4 FDA-approved tyrosine kinase inhibitors are highly effective in treating chronic phase CML. In selecting front-line therapy, a patient’s age, comorbidities, and tyrosine kinase toxicities must be taken into account. For patients with chronic phase CML, the use of the Sokal or Hasford risk stratification scores helps predict outcomes and choose the first-line tyrosine-kinase inhibitor. [11] [12] [11] For chronic phase, CML with intermediate- or high-risk score, second-generation tyrosine kinase inhibitors (bosutinib, dasatinib, nilotinib) may have the additional benefit over imatinib in achieving response milestones. [15] [16] [17] [28]

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Mechanism -- Activation of Cytoplasmic Kinase Domains
agent1_snippets.content: TKDs of RTKs are comprised of a C-lobe, N-lobe, and "activation loop." For a TKD to become functional, both the "activation loop" and N-lobe alpha-C helix must adopt a specific, "active" configuration. In the majority of cases, the release of cis-autoinhibition is required for this to occur. Cis-autoinhibition occurs in several ways. In Ins receptors, a tyrosine on the activation loop occupies the kinase active site, forming a stable configuration that blocks ATP and protein substrate entry.

agent1_snippets.title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Keratitis -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Tyrosine kinases (TK) are proteins exclusively found in cells of multicellular animals. They play a key role as mediators in the signal transduction cascade for regulating cell division and cell death pathways. The TKs can be subdivided into two groups, receptor protein tyrosine kinase (RTK) and non-receptor protein tyrosine kinase (NRTK). The RTK category consists of platelet-derived growth factor receptor (PDGFR), vascular endothelial growth factor receptor (VEGFR), epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), and many more minor receptor proteins. The NRTK are cytoplasmic proteins that have 9 defined families consisting of Fes/Fer, Syk/Zap70, Abl, Tec, Jak, Ack, Fak, Csk, and Src. Four other NRTK exist outside of the defined families, Srm, Rak/Frk, Brk/Sik, and Rlk/Txk. NRTKs are highly regulated proteins with fundamental cellular functions such as apoptosis and cell differentiation. [1] [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Dermatofibrosarcoma Protuberans -- Treatment / Management -- Molecularly Targeted Therapy
agent1_snippets.content: Imatinib inhibits the PDGF receptor and other receptor tyrosine kinases such as c-KIT. Imatinib has the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval for unresectable, recurrent, or metastatic DFSP. [27] Imatinib competitively inhibits adenosine triphosphate (ATP) binding to the PDGFB receptor, slowing down kinase activity, limiting tumor growth, and promoting apoptosis. Patients with the t(17;22)(q22;q13) translocation show a more significant response to imatinib, and thus, screening for this translocation should be performed before initiating therapy. Further studies are necessary to see if patients without the translocation will benefit from imatinib.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
agent2_snippets.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
agent2_snippets.content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). The “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” group, encompassing cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin, belongs to the same protein‑kinase‑inhibitor class and is employed to inhibit kinase signaling in cancer and to modulate immune responses. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically recognized for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Collectively, these agents illustrate how distinct kinase‑targeted drugs are organized within broader therapeutic families and linked to the management of various hematologic malignancies.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
agent2_snippets.content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
agent2_snippets.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
agent2_snippets.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
agent2_snippets.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

agent2_snippets.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib; they inhibit the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase pathway and are employed in hematologic malignancies, with dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib and imatinib mesylát indicated for lymphoid leukemia and ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, another protein‑kinase inhibitor subgroup, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib and target Bruton's tyrosine‑kinase; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia, highlighting BTK inhibition in lymphoid cancers. EGFR inhibitors such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class and exemplify kinase‑targeted therapy beyond leukemias. The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors” also contains agents such as cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin have been identified as potential treatments for acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these protein‑kinase inhibitors—targeting BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases—constitute a network of antineoplastic and immunomodulating drugs that underpin contemporary therapeutic strategies for lymphoid and myeloid leukemias and other cancers.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
agent2_snippets.content: The compound 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent, and within this anticholesteremic category a subcategory called hematologic agents (hematologické látky) is defined. Hematologic agents include drugs such as zanubrutinib, filgrastim, and protamines. Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Ibrutinib is indicated for the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Filgrastim, also a hematologic agent, is used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production, and protamines represent another type of hematologic agent. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors constitute another group of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are examples of EGFR inhibitors. A broader grouping labeled “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” also falls under the protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents class. This group includes cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin have therapeutic activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Overall, the taxonomy links several targeted therapies—BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other protein kinase inhibitors—to their respective clinical indications in hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia, while also situating them within broader pharmacologic categories like anticholesteremic agents and protein kinase inhibitor classes.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
agent2_snippets.content: Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.
